1: κρίμα τῆς πόρνης τῆς μεγάλης — krima (judicial verdict, executed sentence) rather than krisis (process of judgment); the angel announces not an ongoing trial but a completed verdict already pronounced in heaven. πόρνη (pornē, prostitute) carries the full weight of the OT prophetic tradition of covenant-unfaithfulness (Hos 1–3; Isa 23:17; Ezek 16; Nah 3:4), now applied to Rome as the empire that seduced the nations into idolatrous alliance. τῆς μεγάλης — the superlative marks her as the ultimate embodiment of imperial seduction.
2: ἐπόρνευσαν (aorist) — the kings of the earth committed fornication with her: a completed act of political-religious alliance treated as covenant betrayal in prophetic idiom. ἐμεθύσθησαν (aorist passive) — were made drunk: the passive voice is deliberate; the nations were intoxicated by Rome's wine, the seductive power of imperial culture that draws participants into its idolatrous economic system. The wine of passion (θυμός) is not measured desire but consuming ardor that leads to judgment.
3: εἰς ἔρημον — into a wilderness: erēmos is the same word used for the faithful woman's refuge in 12:6, 14; here the wilderness is the prostitute's domain, the anti-Eden where the harlot reigns. The deliberate echo inverts the two women: one flees into the wilderness for divine protection, the other sits enthroned in it. γέμον ὀνόματα βλασφημίας — the beast is covered with names of blasphemy; the programmatic nature of the beast's blasphemy is its claim to divine titles that belong to YHWH alone (the imperial cult's demand for dominus et deus, lord and god). ἑπτά κεφαλάς καί δέκα κέρατα — seven heads and ten horns echo Dan 7:7, 20.
4: πορφυροῦν καί κόκκινον — purple and scarlet: Tyrian purple (from murex snails) was the most expensive dye in antiquity, reserved for emperors and the wealthiest elite; the colors identify the prostitute as the empire itself. κεχρυσωμένη χρυσίῳ — adorned with gold (the cognate accusative intensifies: gilded with gold); the same luxury goods appear in the New Jerusalem's description (21:18–21) but as building material of a holy city, exposing the difference between divine and imperial beauty. βδελυγμάτων — abominations (bdelygmata), the LXX term for idols (Deut 29:17; Ezek 5:11); the prostitute's cup is filled with idolatry, making the economic-religious system of Rome explicitly cultic.
5: μυστήριον — mystery: not "mysterious" but an unveiled secret, the apocalyptic technical term for divine revelation disclosed to insiders (Rom 11:25; Eph 1:9; 1 Cor 15:51; Col 1:26–27). The forehead-inscription echoes both the Roman practice of branding enslaved persons and prostitutes, and the high priest's golden plate inscribed with YHWH's name (Exod 28:36–38); the prostitute is the anti-high-priest, bearing a counterfeit sacred inscription. Βαβυλών ἡ μεγάλη — Babylon the Great: the first empire to destroy Jerusalem and exile Israel provides the cipher for Rome; this identification was transparent to first-century readers (cf. 4 Ezra 3:1–2, 28–31; 2 Bar. 10–11; 1 Pet 5:13).
6: μεθύουσαν (present participle, ongoing state) — the prostitute who made others drunk on her wine is herself in a sustained state of drunkenness, but on blood; grotesque inversion of the intoxication theme of vv. 1–2. τῶν μαρτύρων Ἰησοῦ — those who bore witness to Jesus; martys (witness) was acquiring its specialized sense of one who dies for their testimony, giving us "martyr." ἐθαύμασα θαῦμα μέγα — the cognate accusative construction (I marveled a great marvel) intensifies John's astonishment to the point of literary redundancy, signaling the enormity of what he sees: an empire drunk on the blood of the innocent.
7: ἐρῶ σοι τό μυστήριον — I will disclose to you the mystery: the angel's interpretive role mirrors Gabriel's explanatory function in Daniel (Dan 7:16; 8:15–16); apocalyptic vision requires heavenly exposition for the seer to move from image to meaning. τοῦ βαστάζοντος αὐτήν — of the one carrying/bearing her: the beast supports the prostitute; Roman imperial power sustains the economic-religious system of Babylon. The relationship is parasitic: empire needs its commercial ideology, and the commercial system needs the empire's coercive power.
8: ἦν καί οὐκ ἔστιν καί μέλλει ἀναβαίνειν — was and is not and is about to come up: a direct parody of the divine formula ho ōn kai ho ēn kai ho erchomenos (who is and who was and who is to come; 1:4, 8; 4:8). The beast's pseudo-eternal formula is a corruption of YHWH's genuine eternal nature: where God's eternity is expressed as present-past-coming, the beast's is expressed as past-absent-future rising; the beast's "eternity" is a hollow imitation. εἰς ἀπώλειαν — into destruction: the beast's destination is fixed from the outset of the description, framing the entire vision as judgment-narrative. οἱ κατοικοῦντες ἐπί τῆς γῆς — earth-dwellers: Revelation's consistent term for those who orient their lives by the world-system rather than heaven (cf. 6:10; 8:13; 11:10; 13:8, 12, 14).
9: ὁ νοῦς ὁ ἔχων σοφίαν — the mind having wisdom: the same formula as 13:18 ("here is wisdom"); an encoded identification requiring discernment beyond surface reading. ἑπτά ὄρη — seven mountains: oros (mountain) rather than lophos (hill), though both can render Latin mons; the seven hills of Rome (Septemontium) were the universal identifier of the city in antiquity (Virgil, Propertius, Cicero all use it). John's audience in the seven cities of Asia Minor would have recognized the identification immediately while the text maintained deniability before Roman censors.
10: βασιλεῖς ἑπτά — seven kings/emperors: identifying the specific sequence has generated extensive scholarly debate; the most probable reading counts from Augustus (five fallen: Augustus, Tiberius, Caligula, Claudius, Nero; one is: Vespasian; one not yet come: Titus), though the seven may be typological rather than calendric. ὅταν ἔλθῃ ὀλίγον αὐτόν δεῖ μεῖναι — when he comes he must remain for a little while: the divine dei (it is necessary/must) constrains even the beast's ally; no imperial reign escapes divine governance of duration.
11: αὐτός ὄγδοός ἐστιν καί ἐκ τῶν ἑπτά ἐστιν — he himself is an eighth and is from among the seven: the beast both transcends and recapitulates the seven-king series; he is not a new entity but the oppressor-principle distilled. The Nero redivivus myth (the widespread expectation, post-68 CE, that Nero would return from death or from Parthian exile) likely underlies the eighth-king language; Domitian was widely seen as the "second Nero," combining Nero's cruelty with his demand for divine honors. καί εἰς ἀπώλειαν ὑπάγει — and goes to destruction: the refrain of the beast's destiny.
12: δέκα κέρατα δέκα βασιλεῖς — ten horns = ten kings: from Dan 7:24 (ten horns = ten kings arising from the fourth beast); the ten kings have not yet received their kingdoms (βασιλείαν οὐδέπω ἔλαβον), meaning their authority is still future and contingent. ὡς βασιλεῖς μίαν ὥραν — as kings for one hour: their authority is real but radically time-limited; "one hour" contrasts with the Lamb's eternal kingship (11:15). The delegation structure (beast grants authority to the ten) parodies the divine authority structure (God grants authority to the Lamb).
13: μίαν γνώμην ἔχουσιν — they have one resolve/purpose: gnōmē (deliberate resolve, considered opinion, decree); the unity of anti-God forces is ideological, not merely political. τήν ἑαυτῶν δύναμιν καί ἐξουσίαν τῷ θηρίῳ διδόασιν — they give their own power and authority to the beast: the delegated power of human kingdoms is voluntarily surrendered upward to the beast, completing a counterfeit divine-authority chain (God → Lamb → saints vs. dragon → beast → kings → Babylon).
14: πολεμήσουσιν μετά τοῦ ἀρνίου — they will make war with the Lamb (future; the conflict is certain but still coming). νικήσει αὐτούς τό ἀρνίον — the Lamb will conquer them: nikaō is Revelation's consistent victory-verb, appearing in the promise to each of the seven churches (2:7, 11, 17, 26; 3:5, 12, 21) and in 5:5 where the Lamb "has conquered" (enikēsen, aorist: by the cross). The future conquest of 17:14 is the manifestation of the past conquest of 5:5. κύριος κυρίων ἐστίν καί βασιλεύς βασιλέων — Lord of lords and King of kings: the title drawn from Deut 10:17 (God of gods and Lord of lords); here applied to the Lamb, anticipating 19:16 where it is inscribed on the rider's robe.
15: ὕδατα οὗ ἡ πόρνη κάθηται — the waters where the prostitute sits: decoded as λαοί καί ὄχλοι καί ἔθνη καί γλῶσσαι (peoples and multitudes and nations and languages), the fourfold formula for all humanity (5:9; 7:9; 11:9; 13:7). The cosmic ocean imagery comes from Dan 7:2–3 (the four winds stirring the great sea from which the beasts arise); the prostitute's power rests on the mass of humanity subject to imperial domination. The decoded image exposes Rome's universality as the ground of its power and the breadth of its culpability.
16: μισήσουσιν τήν πόρνην — they will hate the prostitute (future): the beast turns on its own ideological system; imperial power devours the commercial-religious culture it created. ἠρημωμένην ποιήσουσιν αὐτήν καί γυμνήν — they will make her desolate and naked: the stripping is judicial nakedness (Ezek 16:37–39; 23:26–29; Nah 3:5 — YHWH strips the unfaithful city before her lovers). τάς σάρκας αὐτῆς φάγονται καί αὐτήν κατακαύσουσιν ἐν πυρί — they will eat her flesh and burn her with fire: cannibalistic destruction echoes the siege-aftermath oracles of Jer 51:25, 58; the burning of Babylon was the climactic act of divine judgment in the prophets.
17: ὁ θεός ἔδωκεν εἰς τάς καρδίας αὐτῶν — God gave into their hearts: the divine sovereignty operating through and over the beast's coalition; the anti-God alliance unwittingly executes the divine decree. The parallel is Isa 10:5–7 (Assyria as YHWH's rod of anger, used against Israel without knowing it). μέχρι τελεσθήσονται οἱ λόγοι τοῦ θεοῦ — until the words of God are completed: teleō (to complete, fulfill, bring to the intended end); the divine word determines the terminus of the beast's permitted authority; the beast operates within a divinely bounded lease.
18: ἡ γυνή ἣν εἶδες ἔστιν ἡ πόλις ἡ μεγάλη — the woman you saw is the great city: the plain identification caps the extended encrypted vision; after 17:9's seven-hills decoding, this is the explicit confirmation. ἡ ἔχουσα βασιλείαν ἐπί τῶν βασιλέων τῆς γῆς — having dominion over the kings of the earth: basileia (kingdom/sovereignty) is the very thing the Lamb's kingdom (11:15; 12:10; 19:6) permanently displaces; the prostitute's political sovereignty is the negative that the new Jerusalem's divine reign reverses.
